back: A plan of action designed to achieve a long-term organisational goal or aim.

the process by which the proceeds of crime are converted into assets which appear to have a legitimate origin, so that they can be retained permanently or recycled into further criminal enterprises.

(UK Proceeds of Crime act)

Is not a fixed state of harmony but rather a process of change in which the exploitation of resources, the direction of investments, the orientation of technological development and institutional change are made consistent with future as well as present needs.
Development that meets the needs of the present without compromising the ability of future generations to meet their own needs.

The variability (volatility) of returns, both positive and negative, that can be quantified through statistical measures (e.g. probabilities, standard deviations and correlations between different returns).

The influence of psychology on the behaviour of financial practitioners.

– Sewell, 2005

The volatility of operating profit or, more specifically, operating cash flows due to the nature of the industry, country and level of operational gearing (i.e. the proportion of fixed to variable operating costs).
The additional variability in the return to equity due to debt (i.e. using financial gearing). Interest on debt is a fixed cost, which leads to more volatile profits for shareholders.
Uncertainty or variability of an investment's returns.
The time it takes for the operating cash flows from a project to equal the initial investment.
The earnings of a project expressed as a percentage of the capital outlay or average investment.
The quantitative result of weighting uncertain events by the probability of their occurrence.
A measure of the dispersion of numerical values around a mean value.
The analysis of changes made to significant variables to determine their effect on a planned course of action.
An indication of the potential monetary loss likely to occur at a given level of confidence.
A monetary measure of all goods and services produced in a country for a specific period (normally a year).
The concept that the combined value and performance of two companies will be greater than the sum of the separate individual parts.
The unraveling of closely connected businesses or processes, usually as a precursor to (and associated with) divestment of non-core businesses to focus on the core business.
The sale of assets or businesses, wholly or in part, often to generate cash or reduce losses.

Splitting a company into two or more independent units and allocating shares in each new company to existing shareholders, assuming that the separate companies will be worth more to the shareholders than the original company.
The "splitting" (they may already be separate subsidiaries) of a business into two or more independent units and the sale of one such unit.
Organisations in the debt market which bring together potential lenders and potential borrowers.
An unconditional promise by the issuer to pay a fixed sum on a set future date to a designated person or the bearer. The bearer is the person who has possession of the instrument.
Short-term unsecured debt issued by high-quality companies. Investors can then trade the paper on the secondary market.
The use of financial instruments, practices and technologies to optimise the management of the working capital and liquidity tied up in supply chain processes for collaborating business partners.
Shares with a fixed rate of dividend that have a prior claim on profits available for distribution.
Bonds issued at a substantial discount to nominal value and redeemable at their nominal value (or above) on maturity.
Bonds issued at a discount to nominal value and which pay no coupon.
Bonds or preference shares that can be converted into ordinary shares.
The investor's right, but not the obligation, to purchase new shares at a future date at a fixed price (the exercise or subscription price).
The creation and issuance of tradable securities (e.g. bonds) backed by the income generated by an asset, a loan, a public works project or other revenue source.
Using a large, evolving, relatively open group of people (usually assembled via the internet) to provide goods, services or finance.
_____ is crowdsourcing to fund a project or venture.
A method of debt financing that enables individuals to lend money to small businesses without an official financial institution as an intermediary.
The first sale of coins (or tokens) in exchange for a payment, which may be made in a cryptocurrency (e.g. bitcoin) rather than a fiat currency (i.e. legal tender).
Large, over-the-counter, international financial markets for deposits, loans and financial instrument denominated in a currency foreign to the issuer.
Long-term tradable bearer bonds issued in a currency that differs from where it was issued.
An option's basic or fundamental price or value. It is the profit that a purchaser could make if the option were exercised immediately.
The difference between the option premium and the intrinsic value of the option.
The change in the price of an option for a change of one cent in the share price. It is calculated as N(d1) from the Black-Scholes model.
The rate of change of delta (the sensitivity of delta) as the share price changes. Gamma (G) = ​​
The measure of value lost over time, usually expressed as the amount of loss per day.
The market exchange rate for buying/selling the currency for immediate delivery.
The exchange rate for buying or selling the currency at a specific date in the future.
A short-term, cash-settled currency forward contract between two counterparties. Also called Synthetic Agreements for Foreign Exchange (SAFEs).
A technique to lock in the value of a foreign currency transaction in terms of the organisation's domestic currency using a combination of investing, borrowing and a spot currency exchange.

A standardised contract between buyer and seller, in which the buyer has a binding obligation to buy a fixed amount (the contract size), at a fixed price (the futures price), on a specified date (the delivery date), of some underlying asset via a recognised exchange.
An agreement between two parties to exchange principal and/or interest payments in different currencies over a stated time period.

An agreement by a bank to enter into a notional loan or accept a notional deposit from a customer for a specified period. The contract is settled based on the difference between the interest rate agreed when the contract is signed and the rate prevailing when the notional loan/deposit is deemed to start.
An agreement by the seller of the cap to pay the buyer the excess of the reference interest rate over the agreed cap rate, based on a notional principal amount.
An agreement by the seller of the floor to pay the buyer the excess of the agreed floor rate over the reference interest rate based on a notional principal amount.

A combination of a bought option and a written out-of-the-money option, both with the same delivery date. It is designed to protect against losses at a reduced cost by "giving away" much of any potential gains.
Are exchange-traded contracts whose value is determined by interest rates.
An exchange between two parties of interest obligations or receipts in the same currency on an agreed amount of notional principal for an agreed period of time.
An option that provides the holder with the right but not the obligation to execute an interest rate (or a currency swap) during a limited period and at a specified rate.

A corporation that operates in at least one country other than its home country. Such companies have offices and factories in different countries and usually have a centralised head office to coordinate global management.

A formal relationship designed to reduce tariffs and non-tariff barriers (e.g. standards and quotas) between bloc members while agreeing on a common external tariff to non-members (e.g. Southern African Customs Union).
Certificates of equal value representing undivided shares in the ownership of tangible assets or the assets of particular projects.
The process of converting illiquid assets into marketable asset-backed securities ("ABS").
A privately organised financial forum or exchange for trading securities.
The spread of economic and financial problems from one country to another.
The system by which companies are directed and controlled.
The potential that a borrower will fail to meet its obligations in accordance with agreed terms.

The risk of loss resulting from inadequate or failed internal processes, people and systems or from external events.

– Basel Committee

A negotiable security evidencing a debt governed by a contract which specifies, for example, the coupon rate, repayment schedule, security (if any), principal value, seniority (if subordinate to other debt) and other covenants.
The time it takes for the present values of the operating cash flows from a project to equal the initial investment.
The occurrence of one event does not affect the occurrence of other events.
The occurrence of one event affects the occurrence of subsequent events.
The occurrence of one event precludes the occurrence of another event (i.e. they cannot both happen).
An objective and independent appraisal of all phases of the capital expenditure process regarding a specific project.

A contract giving one party the right, but not the obligation, to buy or sell an underlying asset (the underlying) at an agreed price on or before a specified date (expiry date). The purchaser/holder of the option can either exercise their right or let it lapse (i.e. not exercise).
An option that gives the holder the right to buy the underlying asset.
An option that gives the holder the right to sell the underlying asset.
The price at which the underlying asset can be bought or sold when exercising the option.

The amount the purchaser must pay to buy the option. Also referred to as the option price, the premium must not be confused with the exercise price. In a perfect market, it should represent the value of the option.
The seller of an option who receives the premium.
Options which can only be exercised on the expiry date.
Options which can be exercised at any time before and on the expiry date.
Options which can be exercised on any one of a pre-set series of dates.
Any options contract listed on a public trading exchange.
Buying an asset with the expectation that it will increase in value.
Selling an asset with the intention of repurchasing it at a lower price.
An option that possesses intrinsic value.
An option that only contains extrinsic value.
An option is at the money if the current market price of the underlying asset is the same as the exercise price.
The difference between the futures and spot prices.
International trade by exchange of goods rather than by currency purchase.
The risk associated with volatility of operating cash flows.
The risk associated with how a company finances its operations (i.e. the split between equity and debt financing).
The risk of failure to meet stakeholder expectations as a result of any event, behaviour, action or inaction, by the organisation, its employees or those with whom it is associated.
A fixed-income instrument that represents a loan made by an investor to a borrower.
The weighted average sum of years in the bond's life, where the weighting factor is the present value of the flows in each year (discounted at the yield to maturity).
The discount rate at which the present value of a project's cash flows is zero
A public shell company that raises funds through an IPO and uses the proceeds to acquire a private company.
A legal entity without assets, active operations or employees.
Buying a large number of shares in a company at the beginning of a trading day, usually aiming to gain a significant percentage of the target's shares before other shareholders can react.
The sale of a portion of assets to a third party in return for cash.
A legal agreement to lend money, secured by a legal charge over the borrower's property.
A business approach that creates long-term shareholder value by embracing opportunities and managing risks from economic, environmental and social developments.

The process of taking environmental, social and governance (ESG) considerations into account when making investment decisions in the financial sector, leading to more long-term investments in sustainable economic activities and projects.
Where a company joins a public market without the issue of any new shares or any marketing of existing shares.

An auction in which the lowest price necessary to sell the entire offering becomes the price at which all securities offered are sold.

– Nasdaq

Any structured financial activity that has been created to ensure a better environmental outcome.

– World Economic Forum
A list of potential swaps kept by a bank or broker to assist in identifying counterparties for investors who want to engage in a swap. It is usually arranged by currency and maturity.
